title: CLAUDE.md — bsync
text: bsync R package analyzing interpersonal / behavioral synchrony continuous dyadic time series. provides C++/Armadillo-accelerated windowed estimators nonstationary lead–lag structure (windowed cross-correlation, windowed dynamic time warping, windowed Granger causality), preprocessing pipeline (PSD-based downsampling guidance, zero-phase smoothing, kinematics, gap imputation, time-bin aggregation), surrogate (pseudo-synchrony) significance testing, peak/valley optima picking leadership-asymmetry indices, theory- data-driven hyperparameter helpers. Full rationale, contracts, surface object spec, resolved defaults DESIGN.md. package must serve newcomers (safe, loud defaults + guidance) experts (every hyperparameter exposed, raw surface accessible, full surrogate machinery) .

title: DESIGN.md — bsync: Behavioral Synchrony Analyses (R package)
text: Provide fast, modern, educational toolkit quantifying interpersonal / behavioral synchrony continuous dyadic time series (motion capture, computer-vision tracking, physiology, acoustic features). package couples efficient, C++-backed estimators nonstationary lead–lag structure () principled preprocessing pipeline, (b) rigorous null-hypothesis testing via surrogate (pseudo-synchrony) data, (c) helper functions vignettes teach user make good, data-driven choices downsampling, smoothing, hyperparameter selection. must accessible newcomers (safe defaults, guidance, clear output) powerful experts (every consequential choice exposed tunable).

title: DESIGN.md — bsync: Behavioral Synchrony Analyses (R package)
text: Prior art (R ecosystem). closest siblings, verified, / don’t : SUSY — Surrogate Synchrony (Tschacher & Meier 2020). canonical implementation exact method behind bsync’s WCC path: windowed cross-correlation dyadic (2-column) series, Fisher-Z transform correlation, mean Z mean absolute Z aggregated within across segments, effect size (ES) computed segment-shuffling surrogates (susy(x, segment, Hz, maxlag = 3, surrogates.total = 500)). Pure R; WCC — DTW, Granger, peak/valley optima, preprocessing tuning layer. mvSUSY — Multivariate Surrogate Synchrony (Meier & Tschacher 2021). Extends SUSY beyond dyad >2 simultaneous series surrogate controls. reference bsync’s group / multivariate direction (§15, M11). rMEA — Motion Energy Analysis synchrony (Kleinbub & Ramseyer 2020). Import/filter/plot MEA time series; MEAccf() windowed lagged cross-correlation increments, per-window best lag, pseudosynchrony via shuffled-surrogate dyads, lead/follow indices (s1_lead/s2_lead). Pure R; MEA-specific sibling bsync’s WCC + optima + leadership layer. crqa (Coco & Dale 2014) — (cross-)recurrence quantification analysis. Reference M10. dtw (Giorgino 2009) — full-series dynamic time warping (windowed/nonstationary). WaveletComp / biwavelet — wavelet coherence. Reference M9. base ccf, signal/gsignal — cross-correlation signal-processing primitives. contribution. bsync , effect, SUSY/rMEA windowed-cross-correlation-plus-surrogate method generalized accelerated, plus : - C++/Armadillo cores inner loops, SUSY/rMEA/crqa pure R. - Multiple estimators behind one surface contract (§4) — WCC windowed DTW windowed Granger — WCC alone, shared optima/surrogate/plot layer. - Matched-null surrogate testing every estimator, circular-shift phase-randomization generators (segment-shuffling IAAFT planned, §6) rather single shuffle scheme. - Peak/valley optima + leadership asymmetry shared, estimator-agnostic layer. - educational preprocessing + tuning layer — PSD downsampling guidance, zero-phase smoothing, kinematics, gap diagnosis/imputation; suggest_wcc_params() (theory-based starting points) autotune_wcc() (empirical surrogate-driven grid search) — siblings provide. Lineage WCC statistics (cite, don’t reinvent). bsync’s default WCC aggregate (mean_abs_z, §9) precisely SUSY’s mean absolute Z; autotune_wcc() effect size SUSY’s ES; M4 peak-per-window option rMEA / Boker et al. (2002) best-lag convention. Docs name lineage explicitly. Honesty caveat state docs print. synchrony surface descriptive estimate moment--moment association, causal model. Granger “causality” predictive precedence Wiener–Granger sense, mechanism. Surrogate p-values test specific null (broken cross-coupling preserving marginal structure); state null method uses.

title: Get started with bsync
text: complete behavioral synchrony analysis five stages: Preprocess — smooth, trim, compute kinematics raw position data. Choose parameters — select window size lag ceiling match signal’s timescales. Estimate — run WCC, WDTW, WGC prepared signals. Test significance — compare observed statistic surrogate null distribution. Quantify leadership — extract optima compute Leadership Asymmetry Index. following example walks five stages using built-sim_dyad dataset, contains 30 seconds simulated 3D motion-tracking data (80 Hz) two individuals. Person leads rhythmic movement early recording, briefly synchronize, Person B takes lead end.

title: Get started with bsync
text: Raw positional data almost always needs smoothing analysis. High-frequency noise amplifies derivatives calculated, contaminating cross-correlations. apply Savitzky-Golay filter compute 1D velocity along Z-axis. preprocessing helpers available bsync: trim_edges() (drop polynomial-smoothing artifacts), aggregate_by_time() / downsample_signal() (reduce sample rate), diagnose_ts_gaps() / impute_ts_gaps() (handle missing values), evaluate_signal_power() (PSD-based downsampling guidance; see vignette("determine-downsampling")).
code: library(dplyr) #>  #> Attaching package: 'dplyr' #> The following objects are masked from 'package:stats': #>  #>     filter, lag #> The following objects are masked from 'package:base': #>  #>     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union  # Smooth raw positions, then compute 1D velocity df <- sim_dyad |>   mutate(     z_A_sm = smooth_signal(z_A, method = "sgolay", window = 5),     z_B_sm = smooth_signal(z_B, method = "sgolay", window = 5),     vel_A  = calc_velocity_1d(time, z_A_sm, fill_edges = TRUE),     vel_B  = calc_velocity_1d(time, z_B_sm, fill_edges = TRUE)   )

title: Get started with bsync
text: two key hyperparameters WCC — window_size (samples per window) lag_max (maximum temporal offset test) — matched dominant behavioral timescale. bsync provides three complementary tools (detailed vignette("choosing-parameters")): suggest_wcc_params() — PSD-driven starting values single dyad. synchrony_multiverse() — sweep full grid view specification curve. autotune_wcc() — select parameters generalize across multi-dyad dataset. quick-start use hard-coded values match 0.5 Hz dominant cycle sim_dyad (window ≈ 4 cycles 80 Hz = 640 samples; lag ≈ half window = 320 samples). practice, always derive signal.

Fisher's Z: 0.9729 #>  #> ── Cross-Correlation Value Distribution ── #>  #>      0%     25%     50%     75%    100%  #> -0.9755 -0.6768  0.0550  0.7400  0.9764 plot(wcc_res, time_step = 1 / 80) # time_step converts frames → seconds

title: Get started with bsync
text: behavioral time series autocorrelated, high cross-correlation can occur purely chance. Surrogate testing builds empirical null distribution breaking dyadic alignment preserving person’s individual signal characteristics. large computationally expensive analyses, set parallel plan running: future::plan(future::multisession, workers = 4). See vignette("surrogate-testing") thorough treatment surrogate methods use phase randomization instead.
code: set.seed(2026)  # Generate 200 circular-shift surrogates (use >= 1000 for publication) null_mat <- generate_surrogate_circular(   y            = df$vel_B,   n_surrogates = 200L,   lag_max      = 320 )  # Compare observed WCC against the null surr_res <- wcc_surrogate(   x                = df$vel_A,   y                = df$vel_B,   y_surrogates     = null_mat,   window_size      = 640,   lag_max          = 320,   window_increment = 80,   lag_increment    = 1 )  print(surr_res)

title: Get started with bsync
text: full WCC surface tells us much synchrony exists moment. track leading, extract lag maximizes correlation within window (pick_optima()), summarize lags continuous Leadership Asymmetry Index (leadership_asymmetry()). title: Choosing Analysis Parameters
text: autotune_wcc() applies two-stage rule: Detectability gate. parameter cell must significant (p < .05 vs. matched-null surrogate) least sig_pct (default 50%) dyads. Cells fail gate excluded. Stability-penalized score. Among gated cells, score median(ES) - iqr_penalty * IQR(ES). rewards high median ES penalizes spread, parameter set consistent across dyads beats one spectacular one dyad weak others. cell passes gate (insufficient data surrogates), warning issued highest-median-ES cell returned fallback.

text: First, load bsync along ggplot2 view diagnostic plots.
code: library(bsync) library(ggplot2)

title: Determining the Downsampling Rate
text: tutorial, simulate 30Hz continuous smile intensity data (e.g., AU12 OpenFace). Genuine facial expressions typically evolve slowly (0.3 Hz 1.5 Hz). add small amount high-frequency white noise simulate micro-jitter automated tracking software.
code: set.seed(42) fs <- 30 # 30 frames per second  # Generate a list of 10 simulated participant signals smile_data <- lapply(1:10, function(i) {   # Randomize interaction length between 45 and 60 seconds   t_end <- runif(1, 45, 60)   t <- seq(0, t_end, by = 1 / fs)    base_freq <- runif(1, 0.3, 1.2) # Genuine slow expression   noise_level <- runif(1, 0.02, 0.08) # Small tracking jitter    # Signal (slow wave) + Noise (rapid jitter)   sin(2 * pi * base_freq * t) + rnorm(length(t), mean = 0, sd = noise_level) })  names(smile_data) <- paste0("Participant_", 1:10)

title: Determining the Downsampling Rate

code: # Evaluate just the first participant single_eval <- evaluate_signal_power(   x = smile_data$Participant_1,   sample_rate = fs,   threshold = 0.95 ) #>  #> ── Signal Power Evaluation ───────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> 95% of signal power is captured below 1.41 Hz. #> ✔ Theoretical minimum rate is 2.81 Hz. #> ✔ Recommended downsampling factor: 6 (Target: 5 Hz | Bin Width: 0.2 sec).  # View the diagnostic plot single_eval$plot #> NULL

title: Surrogate Testing for Significance
text: bsync package provides two primary methods generating null data. Choosing right one depends nature signal.

title: Surrogate Testing for Significance
text: circular shift method takes time series shifts random interval, wrapping end series back beginning. works: destroys temporal alignment Person Person B perfectly preserving exact amplitude distribution power spectrum original signal. use : default recommendation dyadic interaction data. computationally efficient effectively breaks cross-signal dependency keeping “rhythm” individual intact. Important Note: data strong global trends (e.g., person slowly moving across room 60 seconds), simple circular shift might break trend. Ensure use lag_max argument ensure shifts large enough decouple signals.

title: Surrogate Testing for Significance
text: Phase randomization uses Fourier Transform decompose signal frequency components, randomizes phases, reconstructs signal. works: creates signal exact power spectrum (frequency content) original, totally randomized temporal structure. use : “gold standard” highly oscillatory data (e.g., rhythmic postural sway, physiological signals like heart rate respiration). Important Note: method creates entirely new signal frequency components, statistically conservative circular shifting. significantly computationally expensive generate circular shifts.

title: Surrogate Testing for Significance
text: Regardless generation method, logic bsync surrogate pipeline remains consistent: Decouple: Generate matrix \(N\) null time series remove synchrony keeping individual characteristics. Evaluate: Run chosen synchrony metric (WCC, WDTW, WGC) every column matrix. Compare: Calculate empirical p-value proportion surrogate results extreme , extreme , observed result.
code: library(bsync)  # 1. Generate 1000 surrogates using your preferred method null_matrix <- generate_surrogate_circular(   y = dyad_data$person_B,   n_surrogates = 1000 )  # 2. Compare observed results to the null distribution results <- wcc_surrogate(   x = dyad_data$person_A,   y = dyad_data$person_B,   y_surrogates = null_matrix,   window_size = 90,   lag_max = 45 )  # 3. Interpret the empirical p-value print(results)

title: Surrogate Testing for Significance
text: Permutation Count: stable p-value, recommend least 1,000 permutations. Using fewer (e.g., 100) fine rapid exploratory analysis, lack granularity needed report significance levels like \(p < .001\). Power Speed: Surrogate analysis “embarrassingly parallel.” heavy analyses like WDTW, always use future package distribute 1,000+ calculations across machine’s CPU cores. Interpretation: significant p-value mean behavior causal; means observed degree synchrony statistically unlikely occurred two participants moving independently.

title: Windowed Cross-Correlation
text: core, WCC measures linear shape amplitude matching. asks: shift Person B’s signal backward forward time specific amount, closely resemble Person ’s signal?

title: Windowed Cross-Correlation
text: WCC useful? Exploratory Analysis: know two people interacting know typical reaction times, WCC searches wide grid temporal lags discover coordination actually happens. Linear Relationships: highly effective continuous signals like kinematics, facial action units, vocal pitch proportional magnitude movement matters. Stable Local Delays: assumes within given short window (e.g., 2 4 seconds), reaction delay two participants remains relatively constant. avoid WCC? Variable Speeds (Time Warping): one participant performs gesture slowly mimics twice fast, standard cross-correlation fail align properly. cases, Windowed Dynamic Time Warping (WDTW) better choice. Categorical Data: Pearson correlation requires continuous numeric data. appropriate binary states categorical interaction coding. Strict Causal Inference: WCC identifies moved first (lag direction), measures similarity. need rigorous statistical proof Person ’s movement actively predicting Person B’s movement, consider using Windowed Granger Causality (WGC).

title: Windowed Cross-Correlation
text: demonstrate workflow, simulate realistic interaction two participants (Person Person B) captured 30 Hz. generate smooth continuous data simulate bodily motion add random measurement noise. Unlike perfectly stationary interaction, scenario mimics natural turn-taking includes four distinct behavioral phases course 60 seconds: Phase 1 (0 20s): Person leads interaction 10 frames (0.33 seconds). Phase 2 (20 30s): uncorrelated lull participants move independently. Phase 3 (30 46s): Person B takes leads 12 frames (0.40 seconds). Phase 4 (46 60s): Person interjects leads 8 frames (0.26 seconds).
code: library(bsync) library(dplyr)  set.seed(2026)  # Simulation parameters fs <- 30 n_frames <- 1800 # 60 seconds of data  # 1. Generate underlying rhythms (a shared rhythm and two independent rhythms) # We use a moving average to create smooth, human-like motion waves time_seq <- seq(0, by = 1 / fs, length.out = n_frames) shared_base <- as.numeric(stats::filter(rnorm(n_frames + 300), rep(1 / 15, 15), circular = TRUE)) indep_base_A <- as.numeric(stats::filter(rnorm(n_frames + 300), rep(1 / 15, 15), circular = TRUE)) indep_base_B <- as.numeric(stats::filter(rnorm(n_frames + 300), rep(1 / 15, 15), circular = TRUE))  person_A_raw <- numeric(n_frames) person_B_raw <- numeric(n_frames)  # 2. Piece together the dyadic interaction for (i in 1:n_frames) {   if (i <= 600) {     # Phase 1 (0-20s): Person A leads by 10 frames (0.33 seconds)     person_A_raw[i] <- shared_base[150 + i]     person_B_raw[i] <- shared_base[150 + i - 10]   } else if (i <= 900) {     # Phase 2 (20-30s): Lull / No correlation     person_A_raw[i] <- indep_base_A[150 + i]     person_B_raw[i] <- indep_base_B[150 + i]   } else if (i <= 1400) {     # Phase 3 (30-46s): Person B leads by 12 frames (0.40 seconds)     person_A_raw[i] <- shared_base[150 + i - 12]     person_B_raw[i] <- shared_base[150 + i]   } else {     # Phase 4 (46-60s): Person A leads by 8 frames (0.26 seconds)     person_A_raw[i] <- shared_base[150 + i]     person_B_raw[i] <- shared_base[150 + i - 8]   } }  # 3. Create the raw data frame with added measurement noise dyad_data_raw <- data.frame(   time = time_seq,   person_A_raw = person_A_raw + rnorm(n_frames, sd = 0.05),   person_B_raw = person_B_raw + rnorm(n_frames, sd = 0.05) )

title: Windowed Cross-Correlation
text: Raw kinematic data almost always requires smoothing analysis. , apply Savitzky-Golay filter iron high-frequency measurement noise. However, polynomial smoothing algorithms require surrounding data calculate accurate average. causes mathematical distortion extreme beginning end recording. use trim_edges() drop edge artifacts ensure analysis built strictly stable data. standard rule thumb trim length equal smoothing window.
code: # 4. Smooth the raw data and trim the edges dyad_data <-   dyad_data_raw |>   mutate(     person_A = smooth_signal(person_A_raw, method = "sgolay", window = 15),     person_B = smooth_signal(person_B_raw, method = "sgolay", window = 15)   ) |>   trim_edges(trim_length = 15)

title: Windowed Cross-Correlation

code: wcc_results <- wcc(   x = dyad_data$person_A,   y = dyad_data$person_B,   time = dyad_data$time,   window_size = 90,   lag_max = 45,   window_increment = 30,   lag_increment = 1 )  # View a summary of the results summary(wcc_results) #>  #> ── Windowed Cross-Correlation Analysis ───────────────────────────────────────── #> Total Windows: 53 #> Total Lags Tested: 91 #> Window Size: 90 #> Max Lag: 45 #> Mean Abs. Fisher's Z: 0.452 #>  #> ── Cross-Correlation Value Distribution ── #>  #>      0%     25%     50%     75%    100%  #> -0.8416 -0.2729  0.0133  0.3984  0.9982 plot(wcc_results, time_step = 1 / fs)

title: Windowed Cross-Correlation
text: wcc() reduces full WCC surface single number via statistic argument. Two conventions available: "mean_abs_z" (default): mean |Fisher’s Z| every (window × lag) cell surface. mean absolute Z SUSY toolbox (Tschacher & Meier, 2020) reflects average strength coupling across entire lag range tested. "peak": Within window, maximum |Fisher’s Z| across lags identified (strongest lead–lag coupling moment). per-window peaks averaged across recording. best-lag convention used rMEA introduced Boker et al. (2002). two statistics answer slightly different questions. "mean_abs_z" captures average coupling strength full lag range; "peak" highlights strongest lag coupling window always ≥ "mean_abs_z". Choose based research question. Important: Always pass statistic wcc_surrogate() null distribution built quantity observed value (see Section 4).
code: # Default: SUSY mean absolute Z wcc_maz <- wcc(   x = dyad_data$person_A,   y = dyad_data$person_B,   time = dyad_data$time,   window_size = 90,   lag_max = 45,   window_increment = 30,   statistic = "mean_abs_z" # the default )  # rMEA best-lag convention wcc_peak <- wcc(   x = dyad_data$person_A,   y = dyad_data$person_B,   time = dyad_data$time,   window_size = 90,   lag_max = 45,   window_increment = 30,   statistic = "peak" )  cat("mean_abs_z:", round(wcc_maz$aggregate[[1]], 4), "\n") #> mean_abs_z: 0.452 cat("peak:      ", round(wcc_peak$aggregate[[1]], 4), "\n") #> peak:       2.3383

title: Windowed Cross-Correlation
text: Time series data inherently autocorrelated. , high cross-correlation values can sometimes occur purely chance. test synchronization observed meaningful, evaluate null distribution. bsync package uses two-step pipeline . First, generate matrix “fake” partner data using generate_surrogate_circular(). (deep dive different surrogate methods like phase randomization, see vignette("surrogate-testing")). Second, evaluate real data null matrix using wcc_surrogate(). Accelerating Computation Parallel Processing: Running 1,000 permutations can computationally intensive. surrogate evaluated independently, bsync built seamlessly support parallel processing via future package. simply declaring parallel plan() running analysis, workload distributed across multiple CPU cores, drastically reducing computation time. output provides empirical p-value calculating proportion surrogate Fisher’s Z scores meet exceed observed Fisher’s Z. observed synchrony (Z = 0.4506) substantially higher average chance synchrony (Z = 0.3155) higher 1,000 permutations, empirical p-value reported < .001. statistically confirms coordination observed Person Person B driven genuine interactive behavior rather natural autocorrelation signals.
code: # 1. Set up a parallel plan (e.g., using 4 CPU cores) library(future) plan(multisession, workers = 4)  # 2. Generate 1000 surrogate time series for Person B surrogate_matrix <- generate_surrogate_circular(   y = dyad_data$person_B,   n_surrogates = 1000,   lag_max = 45 )  # 3. Evaluate the observed WCC against the null distribution surrogate_results <- wcc_surrogate(   x = dyad_data$person_A,   y = dyad_data$person_B,   y_surrogates = surrogate_matrix,   time = dyad_data$time,   window_size = 90,   lag_max = 45,   window_increment = 30,   lag_increment = 1 )  # 4. Return to sequential processing when finished plan(sequential)  print(surrogate_results) #> ── WCC Surrogate Analysis (Pseudo-Synchrony) ─────────────────────────────────── #> Permutations: 1000 #> Observed Mean Abs. Fisher's Z: 0.452 #> Average Null Mean Abs. Fisher's Z: 0.3175 #> Empirical p-value: < 0.001 #> ✔ Observed synchrony is significantly greater than chance.

title: Windowed Cross-Correlation
text: Choosing right L_size crucial successful local search. L_size must odd integer, defines width neighborhood (frames) algorithm evaluates confirm local maximum. delicate balance strike: L_size small (e.g., 3): algorithm might get trapped tiny noise ripples near lag zero completely miss true interactive response. L_size large (e.g., 45): algorithm might skip fast, immediate interactive response lock onto delayed, mathematically stronger, theoretically irrelevant rhythm. good rule thumb set L_size capture roughly 0.1 0.5 seconds data. 30 Hz, L_size 5 15 frames usually great starting point. pick_optima() computationally lightweight, recommend experimenting several values visually comparing plots.

previous_headings: 1. What is Windowed Dynamic Time Warping?
title: Windowed Dynamic Time Warping
text: WDTW useful? Variable Execution Speeds: ideal complex behavioral mimicry participants perform matching actions different paces (e.g., one person smiles quickly produces slow, drawn-smile). Shape Timing: structural pattern behavior theoretically important rigid frame--frame timing. avoid WDTW? Exact Duration Matters: WDTW achieves alignment stretching compressing time axis, inherently distorts temporal reality interaction. need know exactly many milliseconds separate two behaviors, WCC safer choice. Highly Noisy Data: WDTW highly sensitive extreme outliers high-frequency noise. data properly smoothed, algorithm may aggressively warp time axis match random noise spikes rather underlying behavioral signal. Strict Causal Inference: Like WCC, WDTW measures alignment similarity. statistically prove one signal causing , requires predictive methods like Windowed Granger Causality.

title: Windowed Dynamic Time Warping
text: demonstrate workflow, simulate interaction two participants (Person Person B) captured 30 Hz. generate smooth continuous data simulate bodily motion. scenario, Person leads interaction 15 frames (0.5 seconds) start. course 60 seconds, dynamic smoothly transitions Person B leads 15 frames end.
code: library(bsync)  set.seed(2026)  # Simulation parameters fs <- 30 n_frames <- 1800 # 60 seconds of data  # Generate a smooth base signal using a 30-frame moving average. raw_noise <- rnorm(n_frames + 300) base_signal <- stats::filter(raw_noise, rep(1 / 30, 30), circular = TRUE) base_signal <- as.numeric(base_signal)  person_A <- numeric(n_frames) person_B <- numeric(n_frames)  # Continuous lag shift: Person A leads by 15 frames, smoothly transitioning to B leading lag_shifts <- round(seq(15, -15, length.out = n_frames))  for (i in 1:n_frames) {   idx_A <- 150 + i   idx_B <- 150 + i - lag_shifts[i]    # Add slight independent noise to mimic realistic measurement error   person_A[i] <- base_signal[idx_A] + rnorm(1, sd = 0.05)   person_B[i] <- base_signal[idx_B] + rnorm(1, sd = 0.05) }  dyad_data <- data.frame(   time = seq(0, by = 1 / fs, length.out = n_frames),   person_A = person_A,   person_B = person_B )

title: Windowed Dynamic Time Warping
text: data ready, can run primary wdtw() function. function slides window across time series calculates DTW alignment distance various lags within window. strongly recommend setting scale_method either "global" "local". DTW distances highly sensitive scale input variables. Standardizing data ensures resulting cost matrix driven structural shape behaviors rather arbitrary measurement units. highly non-stationary data baselines drift significantly time, consider using "local". distance_metric defaults "L2" (squared Euclidean distance), standard DTW, "L1" (Manhattan) also available. wdtw() function returns list object class wdtw_res containing results data frame, overall mean distance, input settings.
code: wdtw_results <- wdtw(   x = dyad_data$person_A,   y = dyad_data$person_B,   time = dyad_data$time,   window_size = 90,   lag_max = 45,   window_increment = 30,   lag_increment = 1,   scale_method = "global",   distance_metric = "L2" )  # View a summary of the results print(wdtw_results) #>  #> ── Windowed Dynamic Time Warping Analysis ────────────────────────────────────── #> Total Windows: 54 #> Total Lags Tested: 91 #> Window Size: 90 #> Max Lag: 45 #> Scale Method: global #> Distance Metric: L2 #> Overall Mean Distance: 27.043

title: Windowed Dynamic Time Warping
text: DTW alignment algorithm, almost always find path align two signals, resulting distance cost. determine distance significantly smaller (.e., better aligned) expected chance, use surrogate testing. First, generate null matrix using phase randomization, highly recommended continuous, oscillatory data. , pass wdtw_surrogate(). Managing “DTW Wall”: Windowed Dynamic Time Warping notoriously computationally heavy calculates full cost matrix every possible alignment every window. Running 1,000 permutations sequentially can take long time. handle , bsync offers two solutions: 1. Parallel Processing: loading future package setting plan(), bsync automatically distributes 1,000 permutations across available CPU cores. 2. Fast Method: exploratory analysis, setting fast_method = TRUE evaluates surrogates lag 0. reduces computation time roughly 99 percent still providing reasonable estimate chance alignment. final publications, always run full grid (fast_method = FALSE). output gives us empirical p-value calculating proportion surrogate distance costs less equal observed distance cost. WDTW calculates distance metric rather correlation, lower values indicate stronger synchronization. observed cost (27.16) less half average chance cost (57.25) lower 1,000 phase-randomized permutations. Therefore, empirical p-value reported < 0.001. statistically confirms shape matching observed Person Person B driven genuine interactive behavior. Notice WDTW, smaller cost indicates stronger synchrony. print method automatically adjusts logic test observed cost significantly lower null distribution.
code: # 1. Distribute the workload across 4 CPU cores library(future) plan(multisession, workers = 4)  # 2. Generate null data preserving the amplitude and power spectrum surrogate_matrix <- generate_surrogate_phase(   y = dyad_data$person_B,   n_surrogates = 1000 )  # 3. Evaluate the observed distance against the null distances surrogate_results <- wdtw_surrogate(   x = dyad_data$person_A,   y = dyad_data$person_B,   y_surrogates = surrogate_matrix,   time = dyad_data$time,   window_size = 90,   lag_max = 45,   window_increment = 30,   lag_increment = 1,   scale_method = "global",   distance_metric = "L2",   fast_method = FALSE )  plan(sequential)  print(surrogate_results) #> ── WDTW Surrogate Analysis (Pseudo-Synchrony) ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Permutations: 1000 #> Observed Mean Cost: 27.1612 #> Average Null Cost: 57.2509 #> Empirical p-value: < 0.001 #> ✔ Observed cost is significantly lower than chance (stronger alignment).

title: Windowed Granger Causality
text: Granger Causality relies linear autoregressive (AR) models. core logic simple: try predict Person B’s current movement using Person B’s past movement (restricted model). try predict Person B’s current movement using Person B’s past movement Person ’s past movement (unrestricted model). adding Person ’s history significantly reduces prediction error compared using Person B’s history alone, say Granger-causes B. bsync package automatically calculates directions (predicting B, B predicting ) every time window.

title: Windowed Granger Causality
text: WGC useful? Continuous, High-Frequency Data: ideal data like head pose, facial action units, physiological arousal behavior one millisecond highly dependent previous millisecond. Finding “Driver”: already know two signals related, need statistical proof direction information flow. avoid WGC? Categorical Data: Granger Causality uses Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) regression, requires continuous numeric data. fail produce invalid results binary categorical states. Unknown Lags: Unlike WCC, Granger Causality search massive grid find optimal delay. must specify many lags back model look. reaction time participants highly variable unknown, WCC better exploratory tool. Highly Non-Linear Systems: underlying AR models linear, WGC might miss complex non-linear coordination unless sliding windows kept brief enough local interaction approximates linear relationship.

title: Windowed Granger Causality
text: demonstrate workflow, simulate interaction captured 30 Hz. scenario, simulate conversation clear shift conversational dominance. Phase 1 (0 30s): Person drives interaction. Person B mirrors Person short reaction delay 3 frames (100 milliseconds). Phase 2 (30 60s): Person B takes conversation. Person begins mirroring Person B 3-frame delay.
code: library(bsync) library(dplyr)  set.seed(2026)  fs <- 30 n_frames <- 1800 # 60 seconds of data  # Generate smooth baseline movements using a moving average base_A <- as.numeric(stats::filter(rnorm(n_frames + 50), rep(1 / 10, 10), circular = TRUE))[1:n_frames] base_B <- as.numeric(stats::filter(rnorm(n_frames + 50), rep(1 / 10, 10), circular = TRUE))[1:n_frames]  person_A <- numeric(n_frames) person_B <- numeric(n_frames)  for (i in 1:n_frames) {   if (i <= 900) {     # Phase 1: A leads B     person_A[i] <- base_A[i]     # B follows A with a lag of 3 frames and slight measurement noise     person_B[i] <- ifelse(i > 3, person_A[i - 3] + rnorm(1, sd = 0.05), rnorm(1, sd = 0.05))   } else {     # Phase 2: B leads A     person_B[i] <- base_B[i]     # A follows B with a lag of 3 frames and slight measurement noise     person_A[i] <- ifelse(i > 903, person_B[i - 3] + rnorm(1, sd = 0.05), rnorm(1, sd = 0.05))   } }  dyad_data <- data.frame(   time = seq(0, by = 1 / fs, length.out = n_frames),   person_A = person_A,   person_B = person_B )

title: Windowed Granger Causality
text: use wgranger() function analyze interaction. set ar_order 3, meaning model use past 3 frames history predict current frame. use window_size 120 frames (4 seconds) ensure regression model plenty degrees freedom establish statistical significance. slide window forward 15 frames (0.5 seconds) time. summary output provides immediate, high-level view interaction dynamics. programmed simulation perfectly even split leadership, summary show Person significantly predicted Person B roughly 50% time, Person B significantly predicted Person 50%.
code: wgc_results <- wgranger(   x = dyad_data$person_A,   y = dyad_data$person_B,   time = dyad_data$time,   window_size = 120,   ar_order = 3,   window_increment = 15 )  # View a statistical breakdown of the analysis summary(wgc_results) #>  #> ── Windowed Granger Causality Analysis ───────────────────────────────────────── #> Total Windows: 112 #> Window Size: 120 #> AR Order (Lags): 3 #>  #> ── Significance Summary (p < 0.05) ── #>  #> • 'x' significantly predicts 'y' in 59 windows (52.7%) #> • 'y' significantly predicts 'x' in 66 windows (58.9%)

title: Windowed Granger Causality
text: wgranger() function provides p-values individual windows based F-distribution, time-series data often violates assumptions parametric tests. robust evaluation overall interaction, can test mean F-statistics empirical null distribution. Accelerating Computation Parallel Processing: Solving thousands autoregressive linear models across 1,000 permutations can slow large datasets. bsync natively supports parallel processing future ecosystem. analyzing heavy data, simply running future::plan(future::multisession) function instantly distribute workload across machine’s hardware. short demonstration, standard sequential processing fast enough. output provides empirical p-values calculating proportion surrogate mean F-statistics meet exceed observed mean F-statistics. simulated interaction, leadership split evenly: Person led first half Person B led second half. result, analysis correctly identifies significant predictive power directions course overall interaction. observed F-statistics (161.27 134.87) vastly outperform chance baseline (roughly 1.87), resulting highly significant empirical p-values x -> y y -> x directions.
code: # 1. Generate null data surrogate_matrix <- generate_surrogate_circular(   y = dyad_data$person_B,   n_surrogates = 1000 )  # 2. Evaluate the observed F-statistics surrogate_results <- wgranger_surrogate(   x = dyad_data$person_A,   y = dyad_data$person_B,   y_surrogates = surrogate_matrix,   time = dyad_data$time,   window_size = 120,   ar_order = 3,   window_increment = 15 )  print(surrogate_results) #>  #> ── Windowed Granger Surrogate Analysis ───────────────────────────────────────── #>  #> ── Direction: x -> y ── #>  #> Permutations: 1000 #> Observed Mean F-statistic: 159.482 #> Average Null F-statistic: 1.867 #> Empirical p-value: 0.001 #> ✔ Predictive power (x -> y) is significantly greater than chance. #>  #> ── Direction: y -> x ── #>  #> Observed Mean F-statistic: 133.1204 #> Average Null F-statistic: 1.8685 #> Empirical p-value: 0.003 #> ✔ Predictive power (y -> x) is significantly greater than chance.

text: goal bsync provide modern, high-efficiency R toolkit analyzing interpersonal behavioral synchrony. traditional cross-correlation assumes association two time series stationary time, many psychological behavioral contexts (interpersonal conversation synchronized movement), lead-lag relationship individuals highly dynamic. bsync allows researchers quantify nonstationary associations using highly optimized windowed cross-correlation (WCC), windowed dynamic time warping (WDTW), windowed Granger causality (WGC), optima-extraction algorithms. Furthermore, package provides complete analytical pipeline behavioral time series. includes robust preprocessing functions time-bin aggregation, zero-phase signal smoothing, kinematic velocity/speed calculations; rigorous hypothesis testing via surrogate null distributions (pseudo-synchrony testing); data-driven parameter guidance tools (suggest_wcc_params(), synchrony_multiverse(), autotune_wcc()) match analysis hyperparameters signal’s timescales.

title: Behavioral Synchrony Analyses
text: following example demonstrates Quick Start WCC pipeline. New bsync? Start Get started vignette full workflow walkthrough, estimator decision table, links every deep-dive article. parameter selection guidance (choosing window_size lag_max), see Choosing Analysis Parameters. use included sim_dyad dataset, contains 30 seconds simulated 3D motion tracking data two individuals. simulation, Person begins leading rhythmic movement, synchronize middle, Person B takes lead end. First, load package prepare data. highly recommend smoothing positional data calculating velocities prevent high-frequency noise amplifying skewing cross-correlations. apply Savitzky-Golay filter calculate 1D directional velocity Z-axis. Next, calculate windowed cross-correlations. Calling summary() resulting object provides clean, formatted overview analysis settings distribution correlation values. cross-correlations calculated, extract specific lags represent optimal association within time window. Summarizing optima object displays extraction metadata alongside overview results. Finally, visualize resulting correlation landscape. underlying heatmap represents cross-correlation values lag elapsed time window. overlaid points connecting lines demonstrate algorithm successfully tracking shifting lag optimum association course interaction.
code: library(bsync) library(dplyr)  data("sim_dyad")  # Step 1: Smooth the raw position data and calculate 1D velocity df <- sim_dyad |>   mutate(     z_A_smooth = smooth_signal(z_A, method = "sgolay", window = 5),     z_B_smooth = smooth_signal(z_B, method = "sgolay", window = 5),     vel_A = calc_velocity_1d(time, z_A_smooth, fill_edges = TRUE),     vel_B = calc_velocity_1d(time, z_B_smooth, fill_edges = TRUE)   ) # Step 2: Calculate Windowed Cross-Correlations wcc_results <- wcc(   x = df$vel_A,   y = df$vel_B,   window_size = 150,   lag_max = 75,   window_increment = 25,   lag_increment = 1,   na.rm = TRUE )  # View the summary summary(wcc_results) #>  #> ── Windowed Cross-Correlation Analysis ───────────────────────────────────────── #> Total Windows: 84 #> Total Lags Tested: 151 #> Window Size: 150 #> Max Lag: 75 #> Mean Abs. Fisher's Z: 1.071 #>  #> ── Cross-Correlation Value Distribution ── #>  #>      0%     25%     50%     75%    100%  #> -0.9984 -0.6830  0.0338  0.7164  0.9986 # Step 3: Extract the Optima (Local Maxima) optima <- pick_optima(   obj = wcc_results,   L_size = 5,   strict_monotonic = FALSE,   search_method = "local" )  # View the optima results summary(optima) #> ── WCC Optima Summary ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  #> ── Completeness ── #>  #> • Total time windows: 84 #> • Valid optima retained: 84 (100%) #> • Optima dropped (NA): 0 (0%) #>  #> ── Lag Directionality (Leadership) ── #>  #> • Positive Lags (x leads y): 41 (48.8%) #> • Negative Lags (y leads x): 40 (47.6%) #> • Zero Lags (Simultaneous): 3 (3.6%) #>  #> ── Optimum Value Distribution ── #>  #>     0%    25%    50%    75%   100%  #> 0.9739 0.9951 0.9971 0.9976 0.9986 # Step 4: Plot the correlation landscape and overlay the shifting optima plot_optima_overlay(   surface_obj = wcc_results,   optima_df = optima )

title: Behavioral Synchrony Analyses
text: Psychological behavioral time series highly autocorrelated, means random noise can sometimes look like genuine interaction. verify observed synchronization statistically significant, can use wcc_surrogate() function. function uses circular shift method misalign two time series, generating null distribution “pseudo-synchrony” preserves natural autocorrelation data breaks dyadic interaction.
code: # Step 5: Run surrogate analysis to calculate an empirical p-value # First generate a null distribution via circular shift set.seed(2026) null_matrix <- generate_surrogate_circular(y = df$vel_B, n_surrogates = 100)  # Then compare observed WCC against the null distribution surrogate_results <- wcc_surrogate(   x = df$vel_A,   y = df$vel_B,   y_surrogates = null_matrix,   window_size = 150,   lag_max = 75,   window_increment = 25,   lag_increment = 1 )  surrogate_results #> ── WCC Surrogate Analysis (Pseudo-Synchrony) ─────────────────────────────────── #> Permutations: 100 #> Observed Mean Abs. Fisher's Z: 1.071 #> Average Null Mean Abs. Fisher's Z: 1.007 #> Empirical p-value: 0.06 #> ! Observed synchrony is not significantly different from chance. #> ℹ Note: 100 permutations may be too few for stable p-values. #> Consider setting `n_surrogates >= 1000` for final reporting.

title: Auto-Tune WCC Parameters for a Multi-Dyad Dataset — autotune_wcc
text: Selects Windowed Cross-Correlation hyperparameters detectable (significant vs. null) stable (consistent) across collection dyads. Internally calls [synchrony_multiverse()] dyad applies gated stability-penalized selection rule via [select_specification()].

title: Auto-Tune WCC Parameters for a Multi-Dyad Dataset — autotune_wcc
code: autotune_wcc(   dyad_list,   sample_rate,   window_sec,   lag_sec = NULL,   increment_pct = 0.1,   statistic = "mean_abs_z",   surrogate_method = "phase",   n_surrogates = 100L,   n_tune_dyads = 30L,   sig_pct = 0.5,   iqr_penalty = 0.5 )

title: Auto-Tune WCC Parameters for a Multi-Dyad Dataset — autotune_wcc
text: dyad_list list data frames lists. element represents one dyad must least two numeric columns (two named list elements `x` `y`) containing two time series. sample_rate Single positive number; sampling rate Hz, used convert `window_sec` `lag_sec` samples. window_sec Numeric vector; window size(s) seconds sweep. Use [suggest_wcc_params()] representative dyad find principled starting range. lag_sec Numeric vector; max lag(s) seconds. Default `NULL` uses `window_sec / 2` per cell (SUSY reliability ceiling). increment_pct Numeric; window increment fraction window size (e.g., `0.1` = 10% step). Default `0.1`. statistic Character; WCC aggregate statistic. Default `"mean_abs_z"`. surrogate_method Character; surrogate generator: `"phase"` (default) `"circular"`. n_surrogates Single positive integer; surrogates per cell per dyad. Default `100`. Increase >= 1000 reporting. n_tune_dyads Maximum number dyads use. `length(dyad_list) > n_tune_dyads`, random sample taken. Default `30`. sig_pct Detectability gate: minimum proportion dyads cell must significant (p < .05). Default `0.5`. iqr_penalty Penalty weight cross-dyad IQR ES. Score = `median(ES) - iqr_penalty * IQR(ES)`. Default `0.5`.

title: Auto-Tune WCC Parameters for a Multi-Dyad Dataset — autotune_wcc
text: named list : `window_size` Selected window size samples. `lag_max` Selected max lag samples. `window_increment` Selected window increment samples. `lag_increment` `1L` (standard lag increment). `window_sec` Selected window size seconds. `lag_sec` Selected max lag seconds. `sig_rate` Proportion dyads selected cell significant. `median_es` Median ES across dyads selected cell. `iqr_es` IQR ES across dyads selected cell. `score` Selection score chosen cell. `n_dyads` Number dyads used tuning. `n_cells_gated` Number cells passed detectability gate. `dyad_multiverses` List `bsync_multiverse` objects, one per dyad.

title: Auto-Tune WCC Parameters for a Multi-Dyad Dataset — autotune_wcc
text: **cross-dyad stability?** parameter set maximizes raw synchrony one dyad may simply match dyad's autocorrelation structure. matched-null surrogate controls autocorrelation within dyad (Invariant 2), *best* parameters also replicate across dyads structurally different signals – hence multi-dyad stability criterion. **Selection rule.** Cells pass detectability gate (significant least `sig_pct` dyads). Among passing cells, score `median(ES) - iqr_penalty * IQR(ES)` across dyads, penalizing spread. cell passes gate, warning issued highest-median-ES cell returned (soft fallback). **Dyad sampling.** `length(dyad_list) > n_tune_dyads`, random sample `n_tune_dyads` dyads used speed; call `set.seed()` beforehand reproducibility.

title: bsync: Behavioral Synchrony Analyses — bsync-package
text: comprehensive toolkit analyzing interpersonal behavioral synchrony continuous dyadic time series. Provides efficient, C++ backed implementations three windowed estimators: cross-correlation (WCC), dynamic time warping (WDTW), Granger causality, full surrogate (pseudo-synchrony) significance testing layer using circular-shift phase-randomization generators. Includes peak valley optima picking, rolling leadership asymmetry index, preprocessing pipeline featuring zero-phase smoothing, time-bin aggregation, gap imputation, kinematic velocity speed calculations. Theory-driven data-driven hyperparameter helpers guide window size lag selection.

Default "sgolay". window integer specifying window size. Must odd number "sgolay". Best practice: Calculate based expected duration target behavior. (e.g., 2-second behavior sampled 5Hz = window 11). sg_order integer specifying polynomial order Savitzky-Golay filter. Must less `window`. title: Suggest WCC Hyperparameters — suggest_wcc_params
text: Derives principled starting values Windowed Cross-Correlation parameters **measured signal** via PSD-based dominant timescale estimation.

title: Suggest WCC Hyperparameters — suggest_wcc_params
code: suggest_wcc_params(   x,   y,   sample_rate,   event_duration_sec = NULL,   max_delay_sec = 3,   overlap_pct = 0.5,   min_window_samples = 20L )

title: Suggest WCC Hyperparameters — suggest_wcc_params
text: x Numeric vector; reference time series. y Numeric vector; query time series (length `x`). sample_rate numeric value indicating sampling rate Hertz. event_duration_sec Optional numeric override dominant behavioral cycle duration seconds. Default `NULL` derives PSD `x` `y` via [evaluate_signal_power()]. max_delay_sec maximum plausible reaction time participants seconds, used set initial `lag_max`. Default `3`. overlap_pct desired proportion overlap consecutive windows (0-1). Default `0.5` (50% overlap). min_window_samples Minimum number samples required window stable correlation. Default `20`.

title: Suggest WCC Hyperparameters — suggest_wcc_params
text: named list `window_size`, `lag_max`, `window_increment`,   `lag_increment`, ready pass [wcc()].

title: Suggest WCC Hyperparameters — suggest_wcc_params
text: **Dominant timescale.** `event_duration_sec` `NULL` (default), function estimates dominant behavioral cycle measured signal via [evaluate_signal_power()]: `event_duration_sec = 1 / primary_cutoff_freq`. Pass numeric value override theoretical estimate. **Window size (4-cycles heuristic).** `window_size = round(event_duration_sec * 4 * sample_rate)` (Boker et al., 2002). Four cycles yields stable within-window correlation across range lead-lag relationships; two cycles (Nyquist minimum) noisy. **Hard constraints applied reported:** `lag_max <= floor(window_size / 2)` – SUSY reliability constraint     (`segment >= 2*maxlag`); beyond lagged windows share fewer     half samples. `window_size <= floor(series_length / 2)` – ensures least two     non-overlapping windows fit series. `window_size >= min_window_samples` – minimum-samples floor     stable correlation estimate. violations produce informative messages, silent changes.

title: Synchrony Multiverse Analysis — synchrony_multiverse
text: Sweeps seconds-specified parameter grid across analytic choices evaluates specification matched-null surrogate test (Invariant 2). headline metric **effect size vs. null** – raw synchrony, autocorrelation inflates.

title: Synchrony Multiverse Analysis — synchrony_multiverse
code: synchrony_multiverse(   x,   y,   estimator = c("wcc", "wdtw", "wgranger"),   sample_rate,   window_sec,   lag_sec = NULL,   increment_pct = 0.1,   statistic = "mean_abs_z",   surrogate_method = "phase",   n_surrogates = 100L,   ar_order = 1L,   scale_method = c("global", "local", "none"),   distance_metric = c("L2", "L1"),   na.rm = TRUE )

title: Synchrony Multiverse Analysis — synchrony_multiverse
text: x Numeric vector; reference time series. y Numeric vector; query time series (length `x`). estimator Character string; windowed estimator: `"wcc"` (default), `"wdtw"`, `"wgranger"`. sample_rate Single positive number; sampling rate Hz, used convert `window_sec` `lag_sec` samples. window_sec Numeric vector; window size(s) seconds sweep. lag_sec Numeric vector; max lag(s) seconds. Required `"wcc"` `"wdtw"`; ignored `"wgranger"`. Capped `window_sec / 2` per cell. increment_pct Numeric vector; window increment(s) fraction `window_size` (e.g., `0.1` = 10% step). Default `0.1`. statistic Character vector; aggregate statistic `"wcc"` . One `"mean_abs_z"` (SUSY) `"peak"` (rMEA/Boker). Ignored estimators. surrogate_method Character vector; surrogate generator(s): `"phase"` (preserves power spectrum) /`"circular"` (preserves autocorrelation). n_surrogates Single positive integer; number surrogates per cell. Default `100`. Use >= 1000 reporting. ar_order Single positive integer; AR order `"wgranger"`. Default `1L`. scale_method Character string; scaling `"wdtw"`. Default `"global"`. distance_metric Character string; distance metric `"wdtw"`. Default `"L2"`. na.rm Logical; passed `"wcc"`. Default `TRUE`.

title: Synchrony Multiverse Analysis — synchrony_multiverse
text: `bsync_multiverse` object : `$grid` [tibble::tibble()] one row per parameter cell:       specification columns, `window_size`/`lag_max`/`window_increment`       (samples), `n_windows`, `observed`, `null_mean`, `null_sd`, `es`, `p`       (plus `es_yx`/`p_yx` Granger). `$settings` Named list call-level inputs. `$robustness` Named list: `n_cells` (total specifications       grid), `n_valid` (cells produced computable ES; rest       skipped short), `n_significant`, `pct_significant` (      `n_valid`), `median_es`, `iqr_es`, `sign_consistent` (proportion       significant cells ES > 0).

title: Synchrony Multiverse Analysis — synchrony_multiverse
text: **Grid construction.** vector argument (`window_sec`, `lag_sec`, `increment_pct`, `statistic`, `surrogate_method`) crossed full parameter grid. Seconds converted samples per cell; `lag_max` hard-capped `floor(window_size / 2)` preserve statistical reliability. Cells series short silently skipped appear `NA` rows output grid. **Surrogate reuse.** One surrogate matrix generated per unique `surrogate_method` reused across every cell sharing method; surrogate cost multiply grid size. **Effect size polarity.** WCC Granger, higher values indicate stronger synchrony (upper-tail test): `ES = (obs - null_mean) / null_sd`. WDTW, lower distance better (lower-tail test): `ES = (null_mean - obs) / null_sd`. convention ES > 0 = evidence synchrony. **Granger direction.** `estimator = "wgranger"`, two sets statistics returned: primary (`observed`, `null_mean`, `null_sd`, `es`, `p`) refer x -> y; additional columns `es_yx` `p_yx` give y -> x.

code: wcc(   x,   y,   time = NULL,   window_size,   lag_max,   window_increment = 1,   lag_increment = 1,   na.rm = TRUE,   statistic = c("mean_abs_z", "peak") )

title: Windowed Cross-Correlation — wcc
text: x numeric vector containing time series (length `y`). y numeric vector containing time series (length `x`). time optional numeric vector representing timestamps data. Must length `x` `y`. provided, rolling window indices mapped directly timestamps results, highly recommended maintain accurate timelines edge artifacts trimmed prior analysis. Default `NULL`. window_size positive integer indicating size window, .e., number elements window vector. title: bsync 0.1.0
text: New vignette("bsync") (“Get started”) provides full end--end workflow walkthrough using sim_dyad, WCC / WDTW / WGC estimator-choice decision table, reading map six deep-dive articles. now recommended entry point new users. Structured pkgdown site: articles now grouped “Get started”, “Core estimators”, “Going deeper” sections; function reference index organized eight thematic groups (Estimators, Surrogate testing, Optima & leadership, Parameter guidance, Preprocessing x2, Tidy interface, Data). choosing-parameters vignette reworked — retitled “Choosing Analysis Parameters” (WCC-); synchrony_multiverse() / autotune_wcc() / select_specification() examples now run sim_dyad Simonsohn specification-curve plot glance() robustness summary render; WDTW Granger estimator= support shown. Cross-linking: wdtw-workflow, wgranger-workflow, determine-downsampling vignettes now carry “See also / Next steps” footers consistent wcc-workflow. README refreshed: Granger causality added headline scope sentence; parameter-guidance tools (suggest_wcc_params(), synchrony_multiverse(), autotune_wcc()) mentioned overview; “go next” article table added; canned output regenerated reflect current (M4/M5) labels. Bug fix: print.wcc_res print.wcc_surr longer display spurious double colon Fisher’s Z aggregate label. fix also renames label Mean |Fisher's Z| Mean Abs. Fisher's Z avoid cli markup ambiguity.

title: bsync 0.1.0
text: autotune_wcc() rewritten thin wrapper synchrony_multiverse() gated stability-penalized selection rule. Takes dyad_list, sweeps seconds-specified grid, selects parameter cell () significant vs. matched-null surrogate least sig_pct (default 50%) dyads, (b) maximizes median(ES) - iqr_penalty * IQR(ES) across dyads. Interface change: window_sec/lag_sec replace old window_multipliers/ lag_multipliers arguments. select_specification() — new exported helper implements gated stability-penalized rule list bsync_multiverse objects (one per dyad). Can called directly advanced users. New vignette vignettes/choosing-parameters.Rmd documents three-tool parameter guidance workflow: suggest_wcc_params() single dyad, synchrony_multiverse() visualizing specification curve, autotune_wcc() multi-dyad datasets. older wcc-params vignette removed; content (including window-overlap discussion) folded new guide. glance() bsync_multiverse now reports n_cells (total specifications grid) n_valid (cells produced computable effect size); pct_significant taken n_valid. disambiguates grid total number cells actually evaluated.

what: Phase A — Synchrony multiverse + suggest_wcc_params rework
title: bsync 0.1.0
text: synchrony_multiverse() — new function sweeps seconds-specified parameter grid across analytic choices (window size, max lag, increment, surrogate method, WCC statistic) evaluates specification matched-null surrogate test (Invariant 2). headline metric effect size vs. null, raw synchrony. Supports three estimators ("wcc", "wdtw", "wgranger"). Surrogates generated per method reused across every cell sharing method (efficiency seam). Returns bsync_multiverse object ($grid, $settings, $robustness). plot.bsync_multiverse() — Simonsohn-style specification curve: top panel shows effect sizes sorted magnitude significance highlighting; bottom panel choice dashboard showing analytic choices specification used. Uses pure ggplot2 + base grid package; new dependencies. Tidy interface bsync_multiverse. tidy() returns full specification grid tibble; glance() returns one-row robustness summary (n_cells, significance rate, median ES, IQR, sign-consistency); as_tibble() aliases tidy(). suggest_wcc_params() reworked. New signature takes actual time series (x, y, sample_rate) estimates dominant behavioral cycle signal’s PSD via evaluate_signal_power() (pass event_duration_sec override theoretical estimate). Three hard constraints enforced reported warnings: SUSY lag cap (lag_max <= floor(window_size/2)), series-length ceiling (window_size <= floor(n/2)), minimum-samples floor.

what: M5 — Shared windowed-surface + surrogate framework + tidy interface
title: bsync 0.1.0
text: Unified $aggregate slot. three estimators (wcc(), wdtw(), wgranger()) now return named numeric $aggregate place old per-estimator $fisher_z / $mean_distance scalar. WCC returns c(mean_abs_z = ...) c(peak = ...) depending statistic; WDTW returns c(mean_distance = ...); Granger returns c(f_xy = ..., f_yx = ...). bsync_surface superclass. three result objects now inherit "bsync_surface" addition leaf class ("wcc_res", "wdtw_res", "wgranger_res"). enables dispatch shared methods. Shared infrastructure. build_surface_grid(): single source truth grid math w_max = window_size - 1 boundary (Invariant 4); replaces per-estimator copy-paste. validate_series() / validate_window_params(): shared input validators. run_surrogate_engine(): shared surrogate loop used three *_surrogate() wrappers; accepts prebuilt grid surrogate matrix aggregate-compute function (results_df surrogate path, Invariant 7); M6-multiverse seam. build_surface_heatmap(): shared heatmap scaffold plot.wcc_res plot.wdtw_res (axis labels, time_step scaling, zero-lag line, theme). Tidy interface. generics::tidy(), generics::glance(), tibble::as_tibble() methods bsync_surface objects: tidy() returns one row per cell results_df. glance() returns one-row tibble aggregate(s) + key settings (n_windows, window_size, lag_max, etc.). as_tibble() alias tidy(). Two glance() rows can bound dplyr::bind_rows() comparison. External oracle validation (AC0 preflight). Frozen golden values dtw (v1.23-3, symmetric1 step pattern, L1 cost) lmtest (v0.9-40, full-series Granger F-statistics) committed test-external-oracle.R checked every run without introducing live test dependencies. Bug fix — wdtw_surrogate(fast_method = TRUE) window alignment. fast path now evaluates surrogates exactly windows observed lagged surface (edges reserved ends), lag 0. regression interim refactor use lag-free grid shifted windows past series end, -counting windows producing spurious --range NAs. slow (default) path unaffected.

title: bsync 0.1.0
text: wcc() gains statistic argument ("mean_abs_z" | "peak", default "mean_abs_z"). "mean_abs_z" SUSY mean absolute Z (Tschacher & Meier, 2020) — mean |Fisher’s Z| windows lags — reproduces pre-M4 behavior exactly. "peak" rMEA best-lag convention (Boker et al., 2002): per window, maximum |Fisher’s Z| across lags; mean across windows. wcc_surrogate() gains statistic argument. null distribution always built statistic observed value (Invariant 2). Pass value functions. Shared internal helper. functions now call wcc_aggregate(), single internal helper, guaranteeing observed surrogate aggregates numerically identical. Print methods updated. print.wcc_res print.wcc_surr label aggregate line according chosen statistic.

title: bsync 0.1.0
text: WCC core rewritten NA-aware prefix-sum algorithm. calc_wcc_cpp() now preprocesses six masked prefix-sum arrays per lag O(n) evaluates window O(1), replacing prior O(w_max) inner loop. na.rm modes preserved exactly. Speedup typical configurations: 5–25× depending window_size lag_max; see bench/RESULTS.md measured timings. OpenMP removed. prefix-sum speedup makes OpenMP unnecessary WCC core. SHLIB_OPENMP_CXXFLAGS stripped src/Makevars src/Makevars.win; package serial--default fully reproducible platforms. M1: na.rm now honored WCC. calc_wcc_cpp() gains na_rm parameter; na.rm = FALSE wcc() / wcc_surrogate() now returns NA window containing missing value instead silently using pairwise-complete pairs. M1: Window-size semantics fixed exactly window_size samples. w_max now set window_size - 1 C++ boundary three estimators surrogate wrappers; previously window spanned window_size + 1 samples (--one). M1: Short-series robustness. three create_*_df() builders surrogate grid builders now call cli::cli_abort() series short chosen window_size / lag_max; index sequences use seq_len() throughout. M1: evaluate_signal_power() longer auto-prints plot. plot argument removed; call plot() returned "signal_power_res" object instead, eliminating Rplots.pdf side effect non-interactive contexts. M1: Condition style unified R/impute.R R/surrogate_generation.R. Legacy stop() / warning() calls replaced cli::cli_abort() / cli::cli_warn(). M1: leadership_asymmetry() docs now state centered sliding-window semantics explicitly; min_valid validated single positive integer. M1: suggest_wcc_params() now documents 4-cycles-per-window heuristic (window_size = round(event_duration_sec * 4 * sample_rate)) @details.
